@@ -14,7 +14,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e.1","title":"Set up GitHub Actions for website CI","description":"# Task: Set up GitHub Actions for Website CI ## Description Create GitHub Actions workflow for website quality gates. ## Workflow File `.github/workflows/website.yml` ## Jobs ### 1. lint-and-typecheck ```yaml - uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v1 - run: bun install - run: bun run lint - run: bun run type-check ``` ### 2. build ```yaml - uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v1 - run: bun install - run: bun run build ``` ### 3. test (if tests exist) ```yaml - run: bun test ``` ### 4. lighthouse (optional) ```yaml - uses: treosh/lighthouse-ci-action@v10 with: urls: | http://localhost:3000/ uploadArtifacts: true ``` ## Triggers - Push to main - Pull requests to main ## Caching ```yaml - uses: actions/cache@v3 with: path: ~/.bun/install/cache key: ${{ runner.os }}-bun-${{ hashFiles('**/bun.lock') }} ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Runs on every PR - [ ] Lint + typecheck required to pass - [ ] Build verification - [ ] Caching for speed - [ ] Clear failure messages","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:27:08.308979-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T11:48:52.669536-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T11:48:52.669536-05:00","close_reason":"Created GitHub Actions workflow for website CI. Fixed barrel import issues causing SSG build failures by switching to direct component imports. All lint, type-check, and build steps now pass.","labels":["ci","task"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e.1","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:27:08.310651-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e.2","title":"Set up GitHub Actions for installer testing","description":"# Task: Set up GitHub Actions for Installer Testing ## Description Create GitHub Actions workflow that tests the installer on real Ubuntu. ## Workflow File `.github/workflows/installer.yml` ## Jobs ### 1. shellcheck Fast job that runs on every PR: ```yaml - uses: ludeeus/action-shellcheck@master with: scandir: './scripts' additional_files: 'install.sh' ``` ### 2. test-ubuntu-25 Full installer test (may be slow): ```yaml runs-on: ubuntu-latest container: image: ubuntu:25.04 steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Run installer run: | bash install.sh --yes --mode vibe - name: Run doctor run: | su - ubuntu -c "acfs doctor" - name: Verify key tools run: | su - ubuntu -c "ntm --help" su - ubuntu -c "ubs --help" # ... etc ``` ### 3. test-ubuntu-24 Same but on 24.04 LTS for compatibility. ## Triggers - Push to main - Pull requests touching install.sh or scripts/ ## Matrix Testing ```yaml strategy: matrix: ubuntu: ['24.04', '25.04'] ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] shellcheck passes on all scripts - [ ] Installer works on Ubuntu 24.04 - [ ] Installer works on Ubuntu 25.04 - [ ] All tools verified after install - [ ] acfs doctor exits 0","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:27:21.687302-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T12:46:57.410761-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T12:46:57.410761-05:00","close_reason":"Closed","labels":["ci","task"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e.2","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:27:21.688994-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-187","title":"[cleanup] Consolidate duplicate acfs_resolve_selection implementations","description":"## Issue There are TWO implementations of `acfs_resolve_selection`: 1. **scripts/lib/install_helpers.sh** (lines 43-272) - Actively used by install.sh (sourced via detect_environment) - More comprehensive: has SKIP_TAGS, SKIP_CATEGORIES, ACFS_PLAN_REASON, ACFS_PLAN_EXCLUDE_REASON 2. **scripts/lib/selection.sh** (lines 195-315) - Has test file (test_selection.sh) with 10 passing tests - NOT sourced anywhere in install.sh - effectively dead code - Cleaner separation with named helper functions ## Problem - Duplicate code creates maintenance burden - Tests exist for selection.sh but production uses install_helpers.sh - If someone modifies selection.sh thinking it's the canonical implementation, those changes won't take effect ## Options 1. **Delete selection.sh**: Remove the unused file and its tests, keep install_helpers.sh 2. **Migrate to selection.sh**: Move selection logic to selection.sh, source it from install_helpers.sh 3. **Merge best of both**: Combine the clean structure of selection.sh with the features of install_helpers.sh ## Recommendation Option 3: selection.sh has better test coverage and cleaner code structure. Merge the extra features from install_helpers.sh (SKIP_TAGS, SKIP_CATEGORIES, reason tracking) into selection.sh, then source selection.sh from install_helpers.sh. ## Files Involved - scripts/lib/selection.sh (standalone, tested, unused) - scripts/lib/test_selection.sh (tests selection.sh) - scripts/lib/install_helpers.sh (has duplicate implementation, used) ## Priority P3 - Not urgent, code works, but should be cleaned up to avoid confusion.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:35:13.451373-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:49:28.168892-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:49:28.168892-05:00","close_reason":"Removed duplicate selection.sh. Tests now verify install_helpers.sh (production). All 10 tests pass."} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-19d4","title":"[EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework","description":"# Epic: VPS Conceptual Framework ## Problem Statement Users don't understand what a VPS is, why they need one, or how it differs from their laptop. This creates: 1. Hesitation ("why can't I just run this on my laptop?") 2. Confusion about provider account vs VPS instance 3. Anxiety about ongoing costs (note: costs ARE explained, but "why pay at all?" isn't) 4. Mental model gaps that cause problems later ## Background & Reasoning The VPS is the "engine room" metaphor - but we introduce this metaphor AFTER they've already committed to renting one. By then, they've either proceeded on faith or dropped off. Beginners need to understand: - VPS = a computer in a datacenter that runs 24/7 - Your laptop = the remote control - Why this matters: persistent sessions, agents keep running, work from any device - Account vs Instance: signing up for Hetzner ≠ having a running server The region selection and RAM requirements are also opaque. "Pick a region close to you" - why? "16GB RAM" - what does that mean in practical terms? ## User Story As someone considering ACFS, I want to understand why I need a VPS and what it does, So that I can make an informed decision and understand the architecture. ## Success Criteria 1. User understands the laptop ↔ VPS relationship before committing 2. User understands persistence benefit (work continues when laptop closes) 3. User understands provider account vs VPS instance distinction 4. User understands region selection (closer = faster) 5. User understands RAM requirements (more RAM = more agents) ## Scope - Add "Why VPS?" section early in wizard (before rent-vps step) - Clarify account vs instance in rent-vps → create-vps transition - Improve region and RAM explanations with practical context - Use the "engine room" metaphor earlier and consistently ## Dependencies None - can be developed in parallel with Terminal epic ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish for desktop and mobile - Consider an animated diagram showing laptop → internet → VPS - Use Jargon component for technical terms - Ensure explanations don't make the wizard feel longer (progressive disclosure) ## Technical Approach 1. Add "Why VPS?" content to rent-vps page or a new interstitial 2. Enhance create-vps page with better RAM/region explanations 3. Add transition text between rent-vps and create-vps clarifying the distinction 4. Use GuideExplain components for the conceptual content ## Estimated Effort Medium (2 wizard page updates, new conceptual content) ## Priority Justification P1 (High) - Conceptual understanding prevents drop-off and reduces support burden.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:34:28.001115-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:34:28.001115-05:00"} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-19d4","title":"[EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework","description":"# Epic: VPS Conceptual Framework ## Problem Statement Users don't understand what a VPS is, why they need one, or how it differs from their laptop. This creates: 1. Hesitation ("why can't I just run this on my laptop?") 2. Confusion about provider account vs VPS instance 3. Anxiety about ongoing costs (note: costs ARE explained, but "why pay at all?" isn't) 4. Mental model gaps that cause problems later ## Background & Reasoning The VPS is the "engine room" metaphor - but we introduce this metaphor AFTER they've already committed to renting one. By then, they've either proceeded on faith or dropped off. Beginners need to understand: - VPS = a computer in a datacenter that runs 24/7 - Your laptop = the remote control - Why this matters: persistent sessions, agents keep running, work from any device - Account vs Instance: signing up for Hetzner ≠ having a running server The region selection and RAM requirements are also opaque. "Pick a region close to you" - why? "16GB RAM" - what does that mean in practical terms? ## User Story As someone considering ACFS, I want to understand why I need a VPS and what it does, So that I can make an informed decision and understand the architecture. ## Success Criteria 1. User understands the laptop ↔ VPS relationship before committing 2. User understands persistence benefit (work continues when laptop closes) 3. User understands provider account vs VPS instance distinction 4. User understands region selection (closer = faster) 5. User understands RAM requirements (more RAM = more agents) ## Scope - Add "Why VPS?" section early in wizard (before rent-vps step) - Clarify account vs instance in rent-vps → create-vps transition - Improve region and RAM explanations with practical context - Use the "engine room" metaphor earlier and consistently ## Dependencies None - can be developed in parallel with Terminal epic ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish for desktop and mobile - Consider an animated diagram showing laptop → internet → VPS - Use Jargon component for technical terms - Ensure explanations don't make the wizard feel longer (progressive disclosure) ## Technical Approach 1. Add "Why VPS?" content to rent-vps page or a new interstitial 2. Enhance create-vps page with better RAM/region explanations 3. Add transition text between rent-vps and create-vps clarifying the distinction 4. Use GuideExplain components for the conceptual content ## Estimated Effort Medium (2 wizard page updates, new conceptual content) ## Priority Justification P1 (High) - Conceptual understanding prevents drop-off and reduces support burden.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:34:28.001115-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:50:46.64645-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:50:46.64645-05:00","close_reason":"All success criteria met: (1) Laptop ↔ VPS relationship explained as 'remote control', (2) Persistence benefit in tmux/ntm section, (3) Provider account vs VPS instance in password AlertCard, (4) Region selection guidance, (5) RAM requirements with agent counts. EPIC complete."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1j8r","title":"Add key auth vs password explanation for reconnection","description":"# Task: Add Key Auth vs Password Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Post-Install Transition Experience (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-r2np) ## Description The reconnect command uses `-i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519` which is NEW. Users don't understand they're now using key authentication instead of password. They might wonder "where do I enter my password?" or panic when they're not prompted for one. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/reconnect-ubuntu/page.tsx Add explanation above the SSH command: \`\`\`tsx

Notice something different?

This SSH command uses your SSH key (the -i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519 part) instead of a password. The installer set this up for you!

No password needed! When you run this command, you'll connect immediately without typing a password. That's the magic of SSH keys — more secure AND more convenient.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explains the change from password to key auth - [ ] Reassures that no password prompt is expected - [ ] Briefly mentions this is more secure - [ ] Appears before the SSH command - [ ] Friendly, not technical tone ## UI/UX Notes - Frame it as a positive upgrade, not a change to learn - "Magic" language makes it feel like a benefit - Keep it brief — users are eager to reconnect","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:42:42.043314-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:28:00.229279-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:28:00.229279-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented key-auth (no password) explanation above reconnect SSH command"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1of","title":"[codex.5] Update README codex references","description":"## Goal Audit and fix any codex-cli authentication references in README.md. ## Scope 1. Search README.md for any mention of: - OPENAI_API_KEY - codex authentication - agent login 2. Update any incorrect guidance 3. Add correct auth flow description if missing ## Correct Information - codex-cli uses OAuth via ChatGPT Pro/Plus account - Authentication command: codex auth - No API key needed - Browser-based login flow ## Files to Modify - README.md ## Acceptance Criteria 1. No incorrect OPENAI_API_KEY references for codex 2. Clear, correct auth guidance if discussed","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:01.346705-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:43:44.97006-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:43:44.97006-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed README.md codex auth references: API key -> OAuth, set OPENAI_API_KEY -> run codex login","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1of","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ua5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:36.402862-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1u8e","title":"Add tmux/ntm conceptual explanation","description":"# Task: Add tmux/ntm Conceptual Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Daily Workflow & Getting Started Guide (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2cvc) ## Description Users don't understand what tmux is, why they need it, or what ntm does. They see `ntm new myproject` without understanding the underlying concept of persistent sessions. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/launch-onboarding/page.tsx or /learn/sessions Add GuideExplain component: \`\`\`tsx

The problem: When you SSH into your VPS and then close your laptop or lose internet, your terminal session dies. Any running commands stop.

The solution: tmux creates "sessions" that keep running on the VPS even when you disconnect. Think of it like leaving a TV playing in another room — it keeps going whether you're watching or not.

NTM (Named Tmux Manager) makes tmux easier. Instead of cryptic commands, you get simple ones:

  • ntm new myproject — Start a new session
  • ntm attach myproject — Resume a session
  • ntm list — See all your sessions

This is why you can start a Claude task, close your laptop, go to bed, and come back to find it completed. The session keeps running on the VPS.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explains WHY sessions matter (disconnect survival) - [ ] Uses relatable analogy (TV playing in another room) - [ ] Introduces ntm as the simple interface - [ ] Shows 3 core commands - [ ] Explains the "overnight task" use case ## UI/UX Notes - This is conceptual, not just a command reference - The "overnight task" example is compelling - Keep it scannable with short paragraphs - Works well in SimplerGuide or as standalone","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:24.594424-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:25:08.021828-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:25:08.021828-05:00","close_reason":"GuideExplain 'What is tmux and ntm?' implemented with: problem/solution structure, TV analogy, 3 core commands (new/attach/list), and overnight task use case. All acceptance criteria met."} @@ -102,6 +102,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9ntj","title":"TASK: Create /wizard/verify-key-connection page","description":"## Parent Feature agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-t66c (Wizard Password-First Flow Updates) ## Summary Create a new wizard page that guides users to verify their SSH key-based connection works after the installer has set it up. This closes the loop on the password-first flow and proves the key installation succeeded. ## Rationale After the installer prompts for and installs the SSH key, users need to: 1. Disconnect from the password-based session 2. Reconnect using their SSH key 3. Verify it works without password This step is crucial because: - Confirms key was installed correctly - Teaches user the "correct" connection method - Provides troubleshooting if key doesn't work ## Page Content ### Header "Verify Key-Based Connection" ~1 min ### Main Content \`\`\`tsx // Step 1: Disconnect

Step 1: Disconnect

Type 'exit' and press Enter to close your current session:

Your terminal should return to your local computer prompt.

// Step 2: Reconnect with key

Step 2: Reconnect with your SSH key

Now connect using your SSH key instead of password:

// Success indicator
  • No password prompt appeared
  • You see: ubuntu@vps:~$
If you had to enter a password, something went wrong - see troubleshooting below. \`\`\` ### Troubleshooting Section Common issues and fixes: 1. **Still asks for password** - Key may not have been pasted correctly during installer - Re-run installer: it will prompt for key again 2. **Permission denied (publickey)** - Key file permissions might be wrong - Run: \`chmod 600 ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519\` 3. **Connection refused** - VPS may have rebooted - Wait 1-2 minutes and try again ### SimplerGuide Content Explain: - Why we verify this step - What "key-based authentication" means - Why no password is good (more secure, more convenient) ## Technical Details ### File Location \`apps/web/app/wizard/verify-key-connection/page.tsx\` ### Step Number This becomes step 8 in the new flow (after run-installer). ### URL \`/wizard/verify-key-connection\` ### Navigation - Previous: run-installer (or status-check) - Next: accounts ### State Requirements - Needs vpsIP from localStorage (same as ssh-connect page) ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] New page exists at /wizard/verify-key-connection - [ ] Shows exit command first - [ ] Shows key-based SSH command - [ ] Success criteria clearly stated - [ ] Troubleshooting for common key issues - [ ] SimplerGuide explains the "why" - [ ] Proper navigation (prev/next) - [ ] Step number updated in wizardSteps.ts ## Dependencies - TASK: Update /wizard/ssh-connect (page flow order) - TASK: Integrate SSH prompt into installer (key must be installed first) ## Files Modified - apps/web/app/wizard/verify-key-connection/page.tsx (new file) - apps/web/lib/wizardSteps.ts (add new step) - Related pages (update navigation)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:20:51.279962-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T19:40:27.19223-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T19:40:27.19223-05:00","close_reason":"Completed","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9ntj","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hy7z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:21:22.933443-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9ntj","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-egf5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:21:23.092203-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9x2","title":"Wizard: Windows SSH key commands have broken backslashes","description":"apps/web/app/wizard/generate-ssh-key/page.tsx uses windowsCommand strings with single backslashes (e.g., /Users/jemanuel\.ssh\...), which are treated as JS escapes and drop the backslashes. Fix to use double-escaped backslashes so the copied command is valid on Windows.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaCreek","created_at":"2025-12-20T14:03:29.105181-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T14:04:03.163713-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T14:04:03.163713-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed Windows command strings in generate-ssh-key step to properly escape backslashes (so copied commands use /Users/jemanuel\.ssh\... instead of dropping slashes)."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9xt","title":"[ubu.1] Create ubuntu_upgrade.sh library","description":"# Create ubuntu_upgrade.sh Library ## Purpose Core library implementing Ubuntu upgrade detection and execution logic. ## File Location `scripts/lib/ubuntu_upgrade.sh` ## Functions to Implement ### Version Detection ```bash # Get current Ubuntu version as comparable number (e.g., 2404 for 24.04) ubuntu_get_version_number() # Get current Ubuntu version string (e.g., "24.04") ubuntu_get_version_string() # Compare two version numbers (returns 0 if $1 >= $2) ubuntu_version_gte() { local v1="$1" v2="$2"; } ``` ### Upgrade Path Calculation ```bash # Get next available upgrade version (queries do-release-upgrade -c) ubuntu_get_next_upgrade() # Calculate full upgrade path from current to target # Returns newline-separated list of versions ubuntu_calculate_upgrade_path() { local target="$1"; } # Get the latest available Ubuntu version ubuntu_get_latest_available() ``` ### Pre-upgrade Checks ```bash # Run all pre-upgrade validations # Returns 0 if all checks pass, 1 with error details if not ubuntu_preflight_checks() # Individual checks (called by ubuntu_preflight_checks) ubuntu_check_disk_space() # Need ~5GB free ubuntu_check_network() # Test connectivity ubuntu_check_apt_state() # No broken packages ubuntu_check_not_docker() # Upgrades don't work in containers ``` ### Upgrade Execution ```bash # Perform single-version upgrade # Returns 0 on success, 1 on failure ubuntu_do_upgrade() # Prepare system before upgrade (apt update, dist-upgrade) ubuntu_prepare_upgrade() # Setup resume mechanism for after reboot ubuntu_setup_resume() # Cleanup resume mechanism after completion ubuntu_cleanup_resume() ``` ## Dependencies - Sources: logging.sh, os_detect.sh - External: do-release-upgrade, apt, lsb_release ## Design Decisions ### Why query do-release-upgrade instead of hardcoding? Hardcoding version numbers (like "25.10") becomes stale. By querying `do-release-upgrade -c` (check mode), we always know the actual next available upgrade. This future-proofs the code. ### Why check disk space? do-release-upgrade downloads hundreds of MB of packages and needs space for temporary files. Running out of disk mid-upgrade is catastrophic. We require 5GB free (conservative estimate). ### Why not use unattended-upgrade? unattended-upgrade is for security patches within a version, not for full distribution upgrades. do-release-upgrade is the official tool for version upgrades. ## Testing Notes - Test on 22.04, 24.04, 24.10 base images - Verify upgrade path calculation is correct - Ensure preflight checks catch common issues","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:19:52.130051-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:32:12.108731-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:32:12.108731-05:00","close_reason":"Created ubuntu_upgrade.sh with all core functions: version detection, upgrade path calculation, preflight checks, and upgrade execution. All 20 functions implemented and tested."} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9y9x","title":"EPIC: User Discovery & Onboarding Enhancements","description":"# EPIC: User Discovery & Onboarding Enhancements ## Strategic Goal Make it effortless for users to discover what's installed, understand their environment, and continue learning—without requiring internet access or running heavy verification checks. ## Background & Rationale ### The Problem After ACFS installation, users face a discoverability gap: 1. **"What do I have?"** - No quick way to see installed tools, their aliases, or current state 2. **"What can I do?"** - 50+ aliases hidden in zshrc, not easily discoverable 3. **"How do I continue learning?"** - Onboard progress is basic, lessons are passive 4. **"I'm offline, now what?"** - Web wizard requires internet; no local reference ### Why This Matters - Beginners feel overwhelmed by unfamiliar environment - Power users forget aliases they don't use daily - SSH disconnections + reconnections lose context - The "flywheel" concept requires knowing what tools exist ### Current State Analysis - `acfs doctor`: Heavy (60+ checks), verification-focused, not quick-reference - `onboard`: Good lessons but passive, basic progress tracking - `acfs.zshrc`: Contains aliases but users don't read config files - Web wizard: Excellent but requires internet ### Proposed Solution Three complementary enhancements: 1. **`acfs info`** - Lightning-fast quick reference command - Shows: IP, hostname, uptime, installed tools, key aliases, onboard progress - Outputs: Terminal (default), JSON (--json), HTML (--html) - Design: Read state files only, NO verification/testing 2. **Enhanced Onboard Experience** - Progress visualization (percentage, progress bar) - "Try It" sections with expected output examples - Cheatsheet integration for command discovery - Completion celebrations 3. **Command Discovery System** - Categorized alias/command reference - Available via: `acfs help --commands`, `onboard --cheatsheet` - Extracted programmatically from zshrc (not duplicated) 4. **Static Dashboard Generation** (Optional Extension) - Generate HTML from `acfs info --html` - Serve on-demand with `acfs dashboard serve` - Works completely offline ## Success Criteria - [ ] User can see their full setup in <1 second with `acfs info` - [ ] User can discover all available aliases without reading zshrc - [ ] User can see onboard progress at a glance - [ ] User can access reference material offline - [ ] No duplication with existing `acfs doctor` functionality ## Non-Goals - Real-time monitoring dashboard (out of scope) - Persistent web server (security concerns) - Replacing the web wizard (it serves different purpose) ## Dependencies - None (enhances existing infrastructure) ## Downstream Impact - All feature beads in this epic depend on this for context - Implementation order matters (see dependency graph)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-22T14:51:25.132198-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:51:25.132198-05:00"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a1g","title":"Add Tailscale auth guidance to post-install summary","description":"## Task Update the post-install summary in `install.sh` to include Tailscale authentication guidance. ## Current State The installer prints a summary at the end with next steps. We need to add Tailscale auth. ## Implementation In `print_summary()` function, add: ```bash # Check if Tailscale needs auth if command -v tailscale &>/dev/null; then local ts_status ts_status=$(tailscale status --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.BackendState // "unknown"') if [[ "$ts_status" != "Running" ]]; then echo "" echo " 🔐 Tailscale (Secure Remote Access):" echo " sudo tailscale up" echo " → Log in with your Google account" echo " → Then access this VPS from anywhere!" echo "" else local ts_ip ts_ip=$(tailscale ip -4 2>/dev/null) echo "" echo " ✓ Tailscale connected: $ts_ip" echo "" fi fi ``` ## Ordering in Summary The post-install summary should guide users through auth in a logical order: 1. **Tailscale** (first - enables remote access) 2. **Claude Code** (primary coding agent) 3. **Codex CLI** (secondary agent) 4. **Gemini CLI** (uses native Google, likely already authenticated) 5. **GitHub** (if using git) ## Example Output ``` ============================================================ ACFS Installation Complete! ============================================================ Next Steps - Authenticate Your Services: 🔐 Tailscale (Secure Remote Access): sudo tailscale up → Log in with your Google account → Then access this VPS from anywhere! 🤖 Claude Code: claude → Log in with your Anthropic account (Google SSO available) 🤖 Codex CLI: codex login → Log in with your ChatGPT account (Google SSO available) 🤖 Gemini CLI: gemini → Log in with your Google account Pro tip: Use the same Google account for all services! ============================================================ ``` ## Considerations - Only show auth steps for services that need it - Show ✓ for services already authenticated - Keep it concise but actionable","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:58:34.656476-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:45:48.037286-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:45:48.037286-05:00","close_reason":"Tailscale auth guidance added to print_summary() in commit 4ee3080","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a1g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bt5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:03:15.487935-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a9nk","title":"Add 'Why VPS?' conceptual section before rent-vps","description":"# Task: Add 'Why VPS?' Conceptual Section ## Parent Epic [EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-19d4) ## Description Before asking users to rent a VPS, explain WHY they need one. Currently we jump straight to "pick a provider" without context. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/rent-vps/page.tsx Add a section at the top (or consider a new interstitial step): \`\`\`tsx A VPS (Virtual Private Server) is like having a computer that runs 24/7 in a datacenter. Here's why it matters for AI-assisted coding:
  • Always on: Your AI agents keep working even when your laptop is closed. Start a task, close your laptop, come back to results.
  • Persistent sessions: Your work continues exactly where you left off, even if your home internet drops.
  • Work from anywhere: Connect from your laptop, phone, or any computer. Same environment everywhere.
  • Separation: Keep AI coding separate from your personal computer. No battery drain, no cluttering your system.

Think of it as your "engine room" — the real work happens there, and your laptop is just the remote control.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Appears before VPS provider selection - [ ] Explains benefits in user terms (not technical specs) - [ ] Uses the "engine room" metaphor from onboarding - [ ] Addresses "can't I just run this on my laptop?" implicitly - [ ] Works well on mobile ## UI/UX Notes - This should feel informative, not preachy - Benefits should be concrete and relatable - Keep it scannable with bullet points - Consider making it collapsible if users already understand VPS","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:40:51.789633-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:47.591527-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:47.591527-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: rent-vps/page.tsx has extensive GuideExplain term='VPS' explaining why VPS is needed"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-aa1","title":"Implement atomic state file writes","description":"# Task: Implement atomic state file writes ## Context Part of EPIC: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5uu) ## Problem If the system crashes or SSH disconnects DURING a state write, the state file could be corrupted (partial JSON). This would cause the installer to fail on resume. ## Solution Use atomic write pattern: write to temp file, then mv (which is atomic on POSIX): ```bash save_state_atomic() { local state_file="$ACFS_HOME/state.json" local temp_file temp_file="$(mktemp)" # Write to temp file cat > "$temp_file" < "15 minutes of waiting with scrolling text is scary... User thought: > 'Is it stuck? Should I do something? When will this end?'" Users who close their terminal during installation cause failed setups and support burden. Clear progress indicators reduce anxiety and improve completion rates. ## Components ### 1. Progress Header (Always Visible) Add a persistent header that shows: \`\`\` ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ Agent Flywheel Installer v0.1.0 ║ ║ Progress: [████████████░░░░░░░░] 60% (8 of 13 modules) ║ ║ Current: Installing programming languages... ║ ║ Elapsed: 5m 23s | Remaining: ~3-4 min ║ ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ \`\`\` This should update in-place (using \\r or terminal control codes). ### 2. Module-Level Progress Each module shows: - [1/13] Setting up user account... - [2/13] Installing shell (zsh + oh-my-zsh)... - [3/13] Installing programming languages... - etc. ### 3. Time Estimates Based on benchmarks: - User setup: ~30 seconds - Shell: ~60 seconds - Languages (bun, uv, rust, go): ~180 seconds - Dev tools: ~120 seconds - Cloud CLIs: ~90 seconds - Agents: ~60 seconds - Stack tools: ~120 seconds - Database: ~180 seconds (if enabled) - Total: ~12-15 minutes Show "Remaining: ~X min" based on current progress. ### 4. Clear Completion Signal When done: \`\`\` ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ ✓ Installation Complete! ║ ╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣ ║ ║ ║ Total time: 12m 34s ║ ║ Modules installed: 13/13 ║ ║ ║ ║ NEXT STEPS: ║ ║ 1. Type 'exit' to disconnect ║ ║ 2. Reconnect with: ssh -i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519 ubuntu@YOUR_IP ║ ║ 3. Start coding with 'cc' (Claude Code) ║ ║ ║ ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ \`\`\` ### 5. Troubleshooting Guidance If a step fails: \`\`\` ⚠ Warning: Module 'install_rust' had issues Common causes: • Network timeout - will retry automatically • Package mirror busy - usually resolves on retry The installer will continue with other modules. You can run the installer again to retry failed modules. \`\`\` ## Technical Implementation ### Progress Tracking The installer already has phases/modules. We need to: 1. Count total modules at start 2. Track current module number 3. Calculate elapsed time 4. Estimate remaining based on benchmarks \`\`\`bash # Global tracking TOTAL_MODULES=13 CURRENT_MODULE=0 START_TIME=$(date +%s) # Progress function show_progress() { local current=$1 local name=$2 local percent=$((current * 100 / TOTAL_MODULES)) local elapsed=$(($(date +%s) - START_TIME)) local remaining=$((elapsed * (TOTAL_MODULES - current) / current)) 2>/dev/null || 0 # Clear line and print progress printf "\\r[%d/%d] %3d%% | Elapsed: %dm %ds | Remaining: ~%dm | %s" \\ "$current" "$TOTAL_MODULES" "$percent" \\ $((elapsed/60)) $((elapsed%60)) \\ $((remaining/60)) "$name" } \`\`\` ### Terminal Compatibility - Use ANSI escape codes for in-place updates - Fall back to simple output if terminal doesn't support - Handle both interactive and non-interactive modes ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Progress percentage visible throughout installation - [ ] Current module name displayed - [ ] Elapsed time shown - [ ] Estimated remaining time shown - [ ] Clear completion message with next steps - [ ] Failed module guidance (don't panic) - [ ] Works in both SSH terminal and console - [ ] No breaking changes to existing installer logic ## Dependencies - FEATURE: Installer SSH Key Integration - Should integrate progress for key setup step too ## Testing Plan 1. Run full installation and verify progress updates 2. Test with slow network (progress should still work) 3. Test with failed module (guidance should appear) 4. Test in different terminals (iTerm, Terminal.app, Windows Terminal) 5. Test via tmux (should still work) ## Future Enhancements - Web-based progress dashboard (installer pings API) - Sound notification on completion - Desktop notification via terminal hooks","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:17:52.137253-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T19:34:51.998446-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T19:34:51.998446-05:00","close_reason":"Design specification complete. Detailed progress display requirements, terminal compatibility notes, and time estimation logic documented. Closing to unblock implementation task 3u3m.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-b1yk","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fslp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:18:20.725081-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-b9v","title":"Task: Update Step 12 with Learning Hub integration","description":"# Update Step 12 with Learning Hub Integration ## Parent Feature Wizard Integration (mna) ## Goal Update the wizard's final step (launch-onboarding/page.tsx) to prominently feature the new Learning Hub as the primary continuation path. ## Current State Step 12 currently: 1. Removed unused CommandCard import.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-b9v","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bri","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:24:11.18954-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bags","title":"FEATURE: acfs info Quick Reference Command","description":"# FEATURE: `acfs info` Quick Reference Command ## Purpose Provide a lightning-fast command that shows users their complete setup at a glance—without running any verification checks. ## Relationship to Existing Tools ### `acfs info` vs `acfs doctor` - They Are DIFFERENT | Aspect | `acfs info` | `acfs doctor` | |--------|-------------|---------------| | Purpose | "What do I have?" | "Is it working?" | | Speed | <1 second | 5-30 seconds | | Method | Read state files | Execute verification commands | | Output | Display/reference | Pass/fail diagnostics | | Use case | Quick reference, orientation | Troubleshooting, validation | Think of it like: - `acfs info` = Reading your car's dashboard - `acfs doctor` = Running diagnostic tests on the engine ## Proposed Output Format ``` ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮ │ ACFS Environment Info │ ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯ System Hostname: vps-12345 IP Address: 203.0.113.42 Uptime: 3 days, 4 hours Ubuntu: 25.10 (Plucky Puffin) Installed Tools (47/52) ✓ Shell: zsh + oh-my-zsh + powerlevel10k ✓ Languages: bun 1.2.x, uv 0.5.x, rust 1.84.x, go 1.23.x ✓ Agents: claude, codex, gemini ✓ Stack: ntm, bv, cass, cm, caam, slb, ubs, am ○ Skipped: docker, lazydocker, postgresql Quick Commands cc Launch Claude Code cod Launch Codex CLI ntm new X Create tmux session lazygit Visual git interface rg "term" Search code Onboard Progress ████████░░ 7/9 lessons (78%) Next: 07_flywheel_loop.md Run 'acfs doctor' for health verification Run 'acfs info --help' for more options ``` ## Output Formats - Terminal (default): Colorful, human-readable - `--json`: Machine-readable for scripting - `--html`: Self-contained HTML page for dashboard - `--minimal`: Just the essentials (IP, key commands) ## Data Sources (Read-Only, No Execution) - `/etc/hostname`, `hostname -I` (cached) - `~/.acfs/state.json` (installation state) - `~/.acfs/onboard_progress.json` (lesson progress) - `uptime` command (single fast call) - Manifest tool list (from manifest or state) ## Implementation Location - Add to: `scripts/lib/info.sh` (new file) - Register in: `acfs.zshrc` acfs() function - Subcommands: `acfs info`, `acfs info --json`, `acfs info --html` ## Non-Goals - NO verification (that's doctor's job) - NO network calls - NO long-running commands - NO password prompts ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Runs in <1 second - [ ] Shows system info (hostname, IP, uptime, OS version) - [ ] Shows installed tools summary (from state, not verification) - [ ] Shows 5-8 most useful quick commands - [ ] Shows onboard progress with visual bar - [ ] --json flag produces valid JSON - [ ] --html flag produces self-contained HTML - [ ] --minimal flag shows condensed output - [ ] Works offline (no network required) - [ ] Integrates cleanly with existing acfs CLI ## Design Considerations ### Why Read State Instead of Verify? Speed. Doctor already handles verification beautifully. Users need a different tool for "remind me what I have" vs "check if it works." Reading state.json takes milliseconds; running 60 verification commands takes seconds to minutes. ### Why Include Quick Commands? The #1 question after setup is "what commands can I run?" Showing the top aliases directly in info output provides immediate value without users needing to find the cheatsheet. ### Why HTML Output? Enables the static dashboard feature without running a web server. User runs `acfs info --html > ~/.acfs/dashboard/index.html` once, then can serve it when needed or open directly in browser.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T14:52:05.974108-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:52:05.974108-05:00"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bcin","title":"TASK: Add 'Getting Back In' reconnection section to launch-onboarding","description":"## Parent Feature agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-trkz (Post-Installation Onboarding Experience) ## Summary Add a prominent "Getting Back In" section to launch-onboarding that explains how to reconnect to the VPS after closing the terminal, and how to set up SSH config for easier access. ## Rationale The UX audit identified reconnection as a critical gap: > "If users close their laptop, they don't know how to get back to their work" Users who close their terminal and can't get back in will: - Feel frustrated and stuck - Potentially abandon the project - Flood support with "how do I get back in?" questions ## Content Specification ### Section: "Getting Back In" Place this AFTER "Your First 5 Minutes" but BEFORE existing quick reference cards. \`\`\`tsx

Getting Back In

Closed your terminal? Here's how to reconnect:

{/* Step 1: Open terminal */}

1. Open your terminal app

Ghostty, WezTerm, or Windows Terminal

{/* Step 2: SSH command */}

2. Connect to your VPS

{/* Step 3: Resume session */}

3. Resume your session (if using NTM)

This brings back exactly where you left off - including any running Claude sessions!

{/* SSH Config tip */}
💡 Pro tip: Set up SSH config for easier access

Add this to your local ~/.ssh/config file: 

{
\`Host myserver
    HostName \${vpsIP}
    User ubuntu
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519\`
}

Then just type: ssh myserver

\`\`\` ### Visual Design - Blue-tinted card (different from other sections) - Terminal icon for visual recognition - Numbered steps - SSH config in collapsible details ### Important Notes - Use the user's actual IP from localStorage - Show both Mac and Windows commands where they differ - Keep it above the fold if possible (it's important!) ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] "Getting Back In" section exists and is prominent - [ ] Three clear steps: open terminal, SSH, resume session - [ ] SSH command includes user's IP - [ ] NTM commands explained - [ ] SSH config tip in collapsible section - [ ] Works for both Mac and Windows - [ ] Mobile-friendly ## Dependencies - TASK: Add 'Your First 5 Minutes' section (comes before this in the page) ## Files Modified - apps/web/app/wizard/launch-onboarding/page.tsx","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:21:55.828607-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T19:46:09.958363-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T19:46:09.958363-05:00","close_reason":"Getting Back In section added with 3 steps (open terminal, SSH connect with user's IP, resume NTM), collapsible SSH config tip, Mac/Windows commands. All quality gates pass.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bcin","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-96cx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T19:22:22.834242-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bfv","title":"Add tailscale module to acfs.manifest.yaml","description":"## Task Add Tailscale as a module in the ACFS manifest. ## Module Definition ```yaml tailscale: name: Tailscale description: Zero-config mesh VPN for secure remote access category: networking # New category or under 'tools' phase: cli_tools # Install early, before agents enabled_by_default: true install: method: script script: | # Add Tailscale apt repository curl -fsSL https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu/$(lsb_release -cs).noarmor.gpg \ | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg >/dev/null echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg] \ https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu $(lsb_release -cs) main" \ | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/tailscale.list sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get install -y tailscale sudo systemctl enable tailscaled verify: command: tailscale version expected_pattern: "^tailscale" post_install_message: | Tailscale installed! To connect your VPS to your Tailscale network: sudo tailscale up Then log in with your Google account at the URL shown. docs_url: https://tailscale.com/kb/ tags: - networking - vpn - security - google-sso ``` ## Considerations 1. **Ubuntu version handling**: Use `$(lsb_release -cs)` for codename 2. **Fallback for older Ubuntu**: Tailscale supports back to Ubuntu 18.04 3. **Systemd enablement**: Ensure tailscaled starts on boot 4. **Skip if already installed**: Check before re-installing ## Verification After this task: - `acfs --list-modules` shows tailscale - `acfs --print-plan` includes tailscale in cli_tools phase","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:57:40.857933-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:32:04.587508-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:32:04.587508-05:00","close_reason":"Added network.tailscale module to manifest. Generator creates install_network.sh. Module is Phase 5, enabled by default, uses apt repository method."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bq3","title":"Ubuntu Auto-Upgrade Feature","description":"# Ubuntu Auto-Upgrade Feature ## Overview Automatically detect Ubuntu version and upgrade to the latest stable release (25.10) before running the main ACFS installation. The entire process should be **fully automated and non-interactive**. ## Problem Statement Many cloud providers (OVH, Contabo, Hetzner) default to older Ubuntu versions (22.04, 24.04). ACFS works best on the latest Ubuntu, and some packages may not be available on older versions. Currently, ACFS just warns about old versions but doesn't help upgrade. ## Solution Add a pre-installation phase that: 1. Detects current Ubuntu version 2. Calculates upgrade path to target version 3. Automatically performs sequential do-release-upgrade operations 4. Handles reboots with automatic resume via systemd service 5. Continues ACFS installation after reaching target version ## Design Philosophy - **Fully automated**: No user interaction required once started - **Resilient**: Handle reboots, network issues, and failures gracefully - **Observable**: Comprehensive logging for debugging - **Opt-out available**: Users can skip with --skip-ubuntu-upgrade ## Default Behavior With the standard one-liner: ```bash curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe ``` The installer will: 1. Detect Ubuntu version 2. If below 25.10, automatically upgrade (no prompts) 3. Reboot as needed 4. Resume and continue upgrading until at target 5. Proceed with ACFS installation ## Opt-out ```bash curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe --skip-ubuntu-upgrade ``` ## Technical Constraints 1. Ubuntu only allows upgrading to NEXT version (no skipping) - 22.04 → 24.04 (LTS-to-LTS is special case) - 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 (sequential) 2. Each upgrade requires a reboot 3. do-release-upgrade takes 30-60 minutes per version 4. SSH sessions will disconnect during reboot (users must reconnect) 5. Need systemd service for automatic resume after reboot ## Success Criteria - [ ] Detects Ubuntu version accurately - [ ] Calculates correct upgrade path - [ ] Performs upgrade non-interactively - [ ] Creates systemd service for post-reboot resume - [ ] Chains multiple upgrades without manual intervention - [ ] Resumes ACFS installation after final upgrade - [ ] Cleans up systemd service after completion - [ ] Handles errors with clear logging - [ ] Works across SSH disconnection/reconnection","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:18:52.892435-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:17:59.471452-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:17:59.471452-05:00","close_reason":"All sub-tasks complete: ubu.1-ubu.12 implemented the upgrade system, ubu.8 (d7c) documented it. Feature is fully implemented with version detection, path calculation, systemd resume, error recovery, and documentation."} @@ -278,7 +280,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vmg","title":"Fix wizard step 5->6 navigation bug (page reload)","description":"User reported clicking 'SSH into your VPS' button on step 5 reloads the page instead of navigating. Root cause: ssh-connect page redirects back to create-vps if no IP stored. Fixed by: 1) Hiding Next button on step 5 (page has its own validated Continue button), 2) Scrolling to IP input if stepper is clicked, 3) Removed all window.alert() calls. Also fixed Contabo SSH key docs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-21T15:15:41.35079-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:15:48.703623-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:15:48.703623-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed in commits 246d272, d0e852e. Wizard step 5 no longer shows confusing Next button; navigation validates IP presence; window.alert replaced with scroll-to-element; Contabo docs updated."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vqr","title":"Create SSH troubleshooting guide","description":"# Task: Create SSH Troubleshooting Guide ## Description Comprehensive guide for common SSH connection failures. ## Location - Website: Embedded in wizard step 6 - Installer: ~/.acfs/docs/ssh_troubleshooting.md ## Common Issues Covered ### Permission denied (publickey) **Symptoms:** `Permission denied (publickey)` **Causes:** 1. Wrong key file specified 2. Key not added to VPS 3. Wrong username **Fixes:** ```bash # Verify key exists ls -la ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519* # Try with verbose output ssh -v -i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519 ubuntu@IP ``` ### Connection refused **Symptoms:** `Connection refused` **Causes:** 1. SSH service not running 2. Firewall blocking port 22 3. Wrong IP address **Fixes:** - Check IP is correct - VPS provider may need firewall rule ### Connection timeout **Symptoms:** `Connection timed out` **Causes:** 1. Wrong IP 2. VPS not fully booted 3. Network issue **Fixes:** - Wait 2-3 minutes for VPS to boot - Verify IP in provider dashboard ### Host key verification failed **Symptoms:** `Host key verification failed` **Causes:** - VPS was recreated with same IP - Man-in-the-middle warning **Fixes:** ```bash ssh-keygen -R IP_ADDRESS ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All common issues documented - [ ] Clear cause → fix structure - [ ] Commands are copy-paste ready - [ ] Integrated in website step 6","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:39:03.569312-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T11:30:24.161611-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T11:30:24.161611-05:00","close_reason":"Created comprehensive SSH troubleshooting guide at acfs/docs/ssh_troubleshooting.md covering all common issues","labels":["documentation","task"]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vwv","title":"Build ERROR_PATTERNS database with suggested fixes","description":"# Task: Build ERROR_PATTERNS database with suggested fixes ## Context Part of EPIC: Per-Phase Error Reporting (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fkf) ## What to Do Create a database of known error patterns and their fixes: ### Error Patterns to Include ```bash declare -A ERROR_PATTERNS=( ["curl: (7) Failed to connect"]="Network issue. Check: curl -I https://google.com" ["curl: (6) Could not resolve"]="DNS failed. Check: cat /etc/resolv.conf" ["E: Unable to locate package"]="Package not found. Try: sudo apt-get update" ["Permission denied"]="Permission issue. Ensure running as root or with sudo." ["No space left on device"]="Disk full. Free up space: df -h" ["gpg: keyserver receive failed"]="GPG keyserver unreachable. Retry or check firewall." ["Connection timed out"]="Network timeout. Check firewall for outbound HTTPS." ["checksum mismatch"]="Upstream script changed. See GitHub issues." ["rate limit"]="API rate limited. Wait 60 seconds and retry." ) ``` ### get_suggested_fix() Function Match error text against patterns and return fix suggestion. ## Acceptance Criteria - At least 10 common error patterns covered - get_suggested_fix() returns actionable text - Fallback message for unknown errors - Easy to extend with new patterns ## Files to Create - scripts/lib/errors.sh","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:43:59.387392-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:39:10.792761-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:39:10.792761-05:00","close_reason":"Created scripts/lib/errors.sh with 33 error patterns and get_suggested_fix() function. All tests pass."} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vydu","title":"[EPIC] Installation Resilience & Progress Feedback","description":"# Epic: Installation Resilience & Progress Feedback ## Problem Statement The 10-15 minute installation is anxiety-inducing for beginners: 1. **SSH disconnection panic**: If their WiFi hiccups, they lose connection and think they broke the VPS. They don't know the installer keeps running. 2. **Wall of text confusion**: Lots of output scrolling by. They don't know if it's working, failing, or what any of it means. 3. **curl | bash skepticism**: Security-aware users worry about piping curl to bash. The "View source" link exists but isn't prominent enough. 4. **Command gibberish**: `-fsSL` means nothing to them. ## Background & Reasoning Long-running installations are inherently risky for beginners. Any unexpected behavior triggers panic. The installer IS idempotent and resilient, but users don't know that. We need to: 1. Set expectations BEFORE they run the command 2. Explain what they'll see DURING installation 3. Provide clear recovery instructions IF something goes wrong 4. Address security concerns proactively ## User Story As a beginner running the installer, I want to understand what's happening and what to do if something goes wrong, So that I can wait patiently and recover from any issues. ## Success Criteria 1. User knows that disconnection is recoverable (just SSH back in) 2. User understands progress indicators (green checkmarks = good) 3. User knows to wait for "Installation complete" message 4. User can find and understand the install script source 5. User understands the curl command parts at a high level ## Scope - Add disconnection recovery instructions prominently - Add "What you'll see" section explaining output format - Make "View source" more prominent for security-conscious users - Add optional command breakdown for curious users - Consider adding a "If something fails" troubleshooting section ## Dependencies - Epic D: First SSH Connection (user must be connected) ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish for desktop and mobile - Disconnection warning should be AlertCard variant="warning" - Output explanation should use OutputPreview component - Source link should be visually distinct (security/trust badge) ## Technical Approach 1. Add AlertCard about disconnection recovery near top 2. Add OutputPreview showing example progress output 3. Add expandable "What is this command doing?" with breakdown 4. Enhance "View source" with trust badge styling 5. Add GuideCaution for "If installation seems stuck" ## Estimated Effort Small-Medium (1 wizard page enhancement) ## Priority Justification P1 (High) - Installation failure/abandonment means complete user loss.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:35:47.401107-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:35:47.401107-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vydu","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:37:59.599308-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vydu","title":"[EPIC] Installation Resilience & Progress Feedback","description":"# Epic: Installation Resilience & Progress Feedback ## Problem Statement The 10-15 minute installation is anxiety-inducing for beginners: 1. **SSH disconnection panic**: If their WiFi hiccups, they lose connection and think they broke the VPS. They don't know the installer keeps running. 2. **Wall of text confusion**: Lots of output scrolling by. They don't know if it's working, failing, or what any of it means. 3. **curl | bash skepticism**: Security-aware users worry about piping curl to bash. The "View source" link exists but isn't prominent enough. 4. **Command gibberish**: `-fsSL` means nothing to them. ## Background & Reasoning Long-running installations are inherently risky for beginners. Any unexpected behavior triggers panic. The installer IS idempotent and resilient, but users don't know that. We need to: 1. Set expectations BEFORE they run the command 2. Explain what they'll see DURING installation 3. Provide clear recovery instructions IF something goes wrong 4. Address security concerns proactively ## User Story As a beginner running the installer, I want to understand what's happening and what to do if something goes wrong, So that I can wait patiently and recover from any issues. ## Success Criteria 1. User knows that disconnection is recoverable (just SSH back in) 2. User understands progress indicators (green checkmarks = good) 3. User knows to wait for "Installation complete" message 4. User can find and understand the install script source 5. User understands the curl command parts at a high level ## Scope - Add disconnection recovery instructions prominently - Add "What you'll see" section explaining output format - Make "View source" more prominent for security-conscious users - Add optional command breakdown for curious users - Consider adding a "If something fails" troubleshooting section ## Dependencies - Epic D: First SSH Connection (user must be connected) ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish for desktop and mobile - Disconnection warning should be AlertCard variant="warning" - Output explanation should use OutputPreview component - Source link should be visually distinct (security/trust badge) ## Technical Approach 1. Add AlertCard about disconnection recovery near top 2. Add OutputPreview showing example progress output 3. Add expandable "What is this command doing?" with breakdown 4. Enhance "View source" with trust badge styling 5. Add GuideCaution for "If installation seems stuck" ## Estimated Effort Small-Medium (1 wizard page enhancement) ## Priority Justification P1 (High) - Installation failure/abandonment means complete user loss.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:35:47.401107-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:51:30.637585-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-vydu","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:37:59.599308-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-w0q","title":"install.sh: dry-run prints misleading completion banner","description":"In --dry-run mode, install.sh skips all phases but still prints the full 'ACFS Installation Complete' summary. Adjust print_summary (or main) to show a dry-run summary instead, clearly stating no changes were made.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaCreek","created_at":"2025-12-20T13:56:39.819372-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T13:57:24.752126-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T13:57:24.752126-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed install.sh so --dry-run prints a dry-run completion banner (no changes made) instead of the full 'Installation Complete' summary."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-wfi2","title":"Add installation progress feedback explanation","description":"# Task: Add Installation Progress Feedback Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Installation Resilience & Progress Feedback ## Problem During the 10-15 minute installation, users see a "wall of text" scrolling by. They don't know: - Is it working or failing? - What do the colors mean? - How far along are we? - When should I worry? ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/run-installer/page.tsx Add "What You'll See" section: \`\`\`tsx

The installer shows progress as it works. Here's how to read it:

✓

Green checkmarks = Success

That step completed successfully.

!

Yellow warnings = Minor issue

Something optional was skipped. Usually fine.

✗

Red X = Step failed

The installer will retry or work around it.

Lots of scrolling text is normal! Just watch for the final "Installation complete" message. If you see mostly green checkmarks, you're good. \`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explains color coding (green/yellow/red) - [ ] Shows visual examples of each state - [ ] Reassures that scrolling text is normal - [ ] Tells user what to wait for (final message) ## Why This Matters Anxiety during installation leads to premature abandonment or unnecessary panic. Users who understand the progress indicators can wait patiently.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:54:50.363193-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:35.446502-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:35.446502-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: run-installer/page.tsx explains progress messages and idempotent behavior"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-whw3","title":"Update README OS requirement to Ubuntu 25.10","description":"README still references Ubuntu 24.04+ in badges and diagrams. Update to reflect current requirement: target Ubuntu 25.10 with auto-upgrade from 22.04+ (per AGENTS.md).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:52:58.302284-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T18:54:59.003219-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T18:54:59.003219-05:00","close_reason":"Updated README Ubuntu requirement to 25.10"}
